That is the beauty of the course: potential for a lot of benign bogeys if you aren’t careful. The bevy of short par 4s and reachable par 5s scream score ability. But they are also why 5-hour rounds are the norm here. A tip from the local I was paired with: everything moves toward the front gate, and the fence to the left of the first and second holes. And take 10 yards off any shot that plays downhill toward that gate.

It has been a while since I made the trip to Moorpark to play Rustic canyon and it remained a great experience except for one major factor - the greens were so firm that it was extremely difficult to hold them with a shot from any distance over 120 yards. Other than that, the greens putted well and the rest of the course was in great condition. There are a couple of funky holes on the front side (like #7) but if you play here more than every 5 years I am sure that you will figure out the right way to play them. Too bad my occasional great approach bounced over the back of the green or it would have been a great day on the links.

Played a nice Sunday afternoon round. Got paired up with a good group of guys so we had some nice conversations along the way. Being the old timer of the group I hit from the forward tees. This was perfect to be able to keep up with the long ballers. Course is in excellent shape for being in a 1200 year drought. Greens were fast and challenging as always. Fairways were nice and green with enough carry on the tee shots. Pace of play was a bit long at 4 1/2 hours, which is on average for Rustic. My only complaint was the air conditioner on my cart was broken.

I was fortunate enough to play The Dunes around the time of its opening, when it left me in awe. Twenty-odd years later, I was concerned that memory might have made it better than reality, but I need not have worried. If anything, I'm more impressed now than back then. This is indisputably the best course in Australia, and I've played all the contenders. After two nice warmup holes, at every tee thereafter it's hard not to say "Wow" out loud. On most tees, there is a strategic choice to be made. The course follows the landscape, it is tough but fair, and the greens are large and with natural contours. It is a superb test, I wish I could play it more regularly but the remoteness adds to the pleasure. The standout holes below are just examples, there are 18 one could have included.

Barnbougle dunes course sits on the northeast coast of Tasmania a stunning Tom Doak design, laid out across the natural links land right along Bass Strait. And honestly, it feels like the course was always meant to be there. Barnbougle winds its way through hills and valleys, with dramatic elevation changes that offer spectacular views both out across the ocean, and back down into the holes from different vantage points. Even though it’s a relatively new course in the grand scheme of golf history, Barnbougle has already made a profound impact on golfers worldwide. It’s an ideal destination for a golf getaway, with accommodation available right on the property making it perfect for a full golf resort experience. If there’s one criticism, it’s that Barnbougle can be extremely penal. Miss the fairway, and you’re more than likely losing your ball. The dunes are big, the rough is harsh, and stray shots can turn what should be a fun day into a frustrating one especially if you didn’t pack enough golf balls! But if you’re hitting it well, Barnbougle is one of the most rewarding days of golf you can have. The fairways are pristine, the bunkers are deep and bold, and the greens are full of the fun, creative contours that Tom Doak is famous for designs made not just for the scratch player, but for the everyday golfer as well. Personally, I think the front nine is the stronger side. You’re walking right along the ocean, and almost every hole feels like it was made for the camera it’s truly breathtaking.

The course absolutely lived up to its reputation and ranking. Weaving its way through grassy dunes with loads of elevation changes it’s visually stunning and was in immaculate condition. Whilst I’d been told to take plenty of balls I found it quite forgiving off the tee although you need to know your carry distances on numerous holes to ensure you either carry or land short of the long stuff. If you manage that then the real challenge are the approach shots to greens with huge run off areas and the greens themselves. Whilst in superb condition I have never played greens with such slope and undulations. Most greens were massive and so regularly left with huge putts once the contours had taken even the most accurate approach shots. A 2 putt wasn’t a given on any of the holes and so if you had or bettered 36 putts then you’ll have had a day. The next hole often didn’t identify itself until after you’d finished the previous one which is the sort of layout I like best and the whole environment with the sounds of the waves crashing adjacent to some holes is just a fantastic place to play golf. The food in the Dunes restaurant was excellent and the whole set up is just spot on. A bucket list course very happily ticked off!

Stroll down the first fairway at Barnbougle Dunes, with the firm, springy turf under your feet, feel the stiff breeze off the sea and gaze at the huge dunes and gaping, wild-fringed bunkers, and you could easily kid yourself you’re on a traditional British links. Short of having a railway line running down the landward side of the out-and-back layout they seem to have thought of everything. Okay, so there are a few differences - I’ve never seen a kangaroo hopping around in East Lothian or the Kent coast, as I did here after spending a little too long hacking around in one of those aforementioned gaping bunkers, just short of the third green. And there’s no gorse. It’s not native to Tasmania and if there’s one thing this state does diligently, it’s protect the indigenous flora and fauna (I had to hand over two apples from Melbourne after landing at Launceston airport - there’s a bio security dog there that takes his job very seriously). But what there is here is an absolutely glorious round of golf. It’s only been established 20 years or so but is already firmly lodged in the far upper reaches of most authoritative lists of the world’s best tracks. You really don’t have to play many holes before you find yourself agreeing. The terrain is perfect for golf, with big dunes running alongside a glorious stretch of beach on the north coast of Tassie. Designers Tom Doak and Mike Clayton have taken that perfect terrain and created a magical route through it. There’s a terrific variety of holes. A nice, welcoming par five to begin with - a wide fairway, rolling across the dunes, makes the first tee shot a lot less daunting than you’d normally expect at a world Top 100 course. Maybe they took as their inspiration the Old Course at St Andrews, where the first tee looks down on the widest fairway in golf. Although that one is still pretty daunting. The point about the fairways here, though, is that they are mostly pretty welcoming. However, to score well, you’ll need to pick the right line into the flag. Which means tee shot placement is paramount. And on a links course, with the sloping, undulating fairways, the fast-running turf, and the wind playing havoc (there is, apparently, always a breeze here)….well, you might pick a good line off the tee. Whether you end up there is another thing entirely. It’s then, for your approaches into the par fours and fives, that the real genius of the design reveals itself. Because these are massive, rolling greens with huge slopes and borrows, well protected by big, deep bunkers, grassy run offs, false fronts. To get close to the pin requires you to be in position A on the fairway for your approach. Which you might not be if, instead of strategising, you just stood on the tee and blasted it down the wide, inviting fairway. This is a course where you can do that, you can have a lot of fun - but if you want to score, you need to think your way round. It’s a fascinating challenge.

I’ve visited Barnbougle “The Dunes” on multiple occasions now and must say it’s probably at the top of my list for fun. The course has never let me down in conditioning and provided a different aspect on each visit. My favourite holes are 5,7,8,12 and 17.

Have just played The Dunes again after a couple of years and have been fortunate to have played it numerous times with visits to Tasmania in the past. It just reassured it is an experience that every golfer should place on the bucket list, even though it can be visually intimidating, it rewards the smart options and good shots but seriously bruises the ego and golf ball numbers when ego outweighs the ability. The course is in perfect winter conditions with greens magnificent. The design naturally flows with the terrain and the tee box options are excellent to cater for all abilities.
Course was running quicker than ever but true. Was in great shape. Such a fun track.
The 9th will make or break how you think of this course. If you like the 9th green you will like the course if you don’t then you won’t like the course. All 3 layouts are amazing.

reviews.review_title: Benign conditions reveal a dependence on firmness, wind
reviews.review_text: Having played the Black in both peak-winter and deep-summer conditions over the last several years, I can say that the course is at its best when it's firm and fast. It’s an enjoyable play no matter what, but anyone who enjoys the course at its crispy peak will likely leave feeling a little unfulfilled when it’s softer and slower.

This course was absolutely amazing. Conditions were mint. The greens were as large as you will ever see. Lots of different levels etc. a lot of people think the course is too over the top but I don’t agree with that at all. It’s definitely not traditional but that’s what makes it fun. I would highly recommend other than the price lol. U pay for the experience obviously .Nigel was our caddie and he was amazing and knew every break on those greens. .I highly recommend!

Get ready for some serious putting on this quirky and supremely fun golf course. It’s the newest course at Streamsong and it will have you wishing you would have hit at least a couple shots differently after seeing where they end up. Players who are purists might not like the design and gigantic greens. What you will get at Streamsong Black is a big golf course spread across vast sand dunes, huge waste areas, and a few blind shots. Of the three course at Streamsong I rate Black as the funnest. There are some weird holes and crazy greens, but I love it! Gil Hanse did a great job of using this land to put his stamp on it.

Streamsong Black is a one of a kind golf experience that every true golfer needs to do. The course is the 3rd at Streamsong Resort and compliments the Blue and Red perfectly. In terms of the course, be prepared for the biggest greens in the world. The course is wide open with fairways the size of a airport runway. It plays firm and fast and encourages playing the ball on the ground. I feel the Black is the perfect 3rd course for one of the best golf resorts in the world.

After playing Streamsong Black one thing became apparent to me, this is like no other course you have ever played both visually and strategically. The course is sculpted into the sand dunes of central Florida with sand guarding each fairway and green. With wide landing spaces off the tee, open areas just off the fairways, and sand everywhere it was virtually impossible to lose a ball. The far greatest challenge of the course lies in the greens, drastic undulations make putting very challenging. I would recommend a caddie as he made the day a lot more enjoyable both in company and course management. I am already looking forward to my next visit to Streamsong.

Wow, what of course. They say that this was McKenzie’s favorite course and he designed Augusta and Cypress point. He even lived there on the sixth hole and it was cool to see his understated home. This would rank in the top three public courses I’ve ever played.

I had been wanting to play this historic course for many years and finally got a chance. It didn't disappoint. I would say it can be arduous to walk it if you're pushing a cart and it's 82 outside. Not complaining but that kicked my butt! Had a great time being paired up with another friendly player. Greens were fast but true, loved it. I will say it's a bit overpriced. I don't quite get the public full price rate, perhaps it's real estate costs.

Awesome course, perfect condition, challenging but fair layout, friendly staff. although pricey definitely worth it compared to some of the other high price destination golf courses. Would definitely play again!

Pasatiempo is back and better than ever. The two-year restoration project, spearheaded by architect Jim Urbina and superintendent Justin Mandon, painstakingly stripped the greens to their core - the front nine first in 2023 and the back nine in 2024 - and rebuilt them to MacKenzie's original sizes and shapes from 1929. Poa annua has been replaced by Pure Distinction bentgrass that rolls better. The famous course was in the best shape I've ever seen it. The biggest changes on the back nine involved the greens of the two par 3s: the 142-yard 15th and 173-yard 18th. The wild, three-tiered 16th is still a hoot, although its slopes were tweaked to allow for more pin locations.
If you are wondering if this course is worth it, stop. It is. They could charge way more. They don’t need no -member play. We are all lucky to play it. Just pay up, say thank you, and ENJOY.

We've been meaning to play Pasatiempo for a few years, but the course remodel delayed us. Fortunately, 2025 was the year! It's a great track! The greens had recently been punched and sanded, but they were still in really good condition. If you get above the hole...forget about it! As a threesome, we had 9 3-putts but that would have been considerable more if the greens were running faster. I'd definitely play here again.
Amazing course, amazing layout, amazing condition and stunningly amazing ocean views. Go play it!
